skill.name: fiftyone-code-style
skill.description: Guide Python code changes toward FiftyOne's documented style, imports, docstrings, helper patterns, and validation workflow.
skill.summary: Apply FiftyOne Python style conventions when writing or reviewing code.
skill.author: AdonaiVera

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command-execution, weak-crypto, network, and reconnaissance patterns, but review shows they are Markdown formatting, inline documentation examples, and a project homepage link. No malicious intent, prompt injection, credential handling, or data exfiltration was found; the only practical concern is documented local validation commands that users should run in trusted repositories.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged many backticks as Ruby or shell execution. Review shows these are Markdown code fences, inline module names, aliases, and Python examples, not executable skill logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown fences, inline code spans, and tables in a documentation-only skill. There is no runnable script or dynamic execution path in the skill file.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is Documentation Attribution
low_findings.description: The network finding points to a Voxel51 homepage link inside a copyright header example. It does not make a network request or transmit data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears only as reStructuredText-style documentation text. No fetch, curl, webhook, or other network execution mechanism is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Hits Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The reported weak-crypto and system-reconnaissance patterns correspond to ordinary words, Markdown, or guidance headings. No cryptographic operation or host reconnaissance command is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged areas contain prose about code style, docstrings, and checking existing utilities. There is no hash function call, encryption logic, uname command, or environment probing behavior.

low_findings.title: Local Validation Commands Require User Trust
low_findings.description: The skill suggests grep, pylint, black, and pytest commands for code review and validation. These are legitimate developer workflows, but users should run them only in trusted repositories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are clearly documented and hardcoded, with no obfuscation or exfiltration. They still invoke local tools that may execute project-controlled test or plugin code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented Local Shell Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes shell command examples for searching source code and running lint, format, and test checks. This is expected for a coding style guide but should remain user-controlled.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible examples for developer validation and do not include remote downloads, credential access, destructive flags, or hidden execution.

content.value_statement: FiftyOne contributors need consistent Python style across imports, docstrings, helpers, and validation. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code concrete conventions for writing and reviewing FiftyOne code.
actual_capabilities: Organizes Python imports into standard library, third-party, eta, and FiftyOne groups.
Applies common FiftyOne import aliases such as fo, fol, fof, fom, fos, and fou.
Formats function and class docstrings with Google-style Args, Returns, and Raises sections.
Recommends private helper naming, logger setup, lazy imports, and hasattr guard patterns.
Guides contributors to check existing FiftyOne and eta utilities before adding duplicate helpers.
Suggests local validation commands for linting, formatting checks, and targeted tests.
limitations: It is a style guide, not an automated formatter or linter.
It focuses on Python code for FiftyOne and related plugins.
It does not verify behavior without running project tests or review tools.
It does not replace maintainer review for API design decisions.

use_cases.title: Prepare a FiftyOne Pull Request
use_cases.description: Align new Python modules with expected imports, headers, docstrings, logging, and validation steps before review.

use_cases.title: Review Plugin Code
use_cases.description: Check plugin code for FiftyOne naming, helper structure, lazy imports, and reuse of existing utilities.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Contributions
use_cases.description: Give agents and reviewers a shared checklist for consistent FiftyOne Python implementation patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Style Check a Small Change
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a focused patch.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Python change for FiftyOne code style. Focus on imports, aliases, docstrings, logger setup, and private helper naming.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a New Module
prompt_templates.scenario: Create code that follows the module template.
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a new FiftyOne Python module for this feature. Use the standard header, import grouping, logger pattern, Google-style docstrings, and private helper structure.

prompt_templates.title: Reduce Duplicate Utilities
prompt_templates.scenario: Refactor code before adding a helper.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this helper proposal against existing FiftyOne and eta utilities. Identify reusable functions and suggest a style-compliant implementation plan.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Pull Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this pull request for FiftyOne style, lazy import usage, guard patterns, non-fatal error handling, redundant implementations, and validation commands to run.

output_examples.input: A new module has mixed imports, no logger, and short docstrings.
output: Imports should be split into standard library, third-party, eta, and FiftyOne groups.
Add a module logger using the module name pattern.
Expand public docstrings with Args, Returns, and Raises where applicable.

output_examples.input: A plugin imports torch directly and repeats file utility logic.
output: Use a lazy import for the optional heavy dependency.
Check existing FiftyOne and eta utilities before keeping a custom helper.
Keep private helper names prefixed with an underscore.

output_examples.input: A pull request is ready for final review.
output: Run the project lint, format check, and targeted tests in a trusted checkout.
Confirm imports are alphabetized within each group.
Replace bare exception handlers with specific exception types where practical.

best_practices: Use the skill before finalizing Python code for FiftyOne repositories or plugins.
Ask for specific review areas when a change touches imports, docstrings, or optional dependencies.
Run suggested validation commands only in repositories you trust.
anti_patterns: Do not use it as a general Python style guide for unrelated projects.
Do not treat style guidance as proof that code is functionally correct.
Do not run project tests or tooling from untrusted code without review.

faq.question: Does this skill format code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides conventions and review guidance. Use project tooling to format and validate code.

faq.question: Which projects is this skill best for?
faq.answer: It is best for FiftyOne core code, FiftyOne plugins, and code that follows Voxel51 conventions.

faq.question: Can it help with docstrings?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes Google-style function and class docstrings with arguments, returns, and raised errors.

faq.question: Does it install dependencies?
faq.answer: No. It documents lazy import and package check patterns but does not install packages itself.

faq.question: Why is there a low security risk rating?
faq.answer: The skill includes local command examples for search, linting, formatting checks, and tests.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
